text: The laparoscopic ports are positioned as shown, with the surgeon standing on the patient's left side. The gallbladder is grasped at the fundus and traction is applied towards the patient's right shoulder, providing clear exposure of the Calot's triangle anatomy. The surgeon uses their left hand to hold the infundibulum.

The surgeon moves the gallbladder in a leg-like manner, revealing the common bile duct (CBD) and gallbladder. A burst of energy is applied at the peritoneal fold of the gallbladder, clearing away fat and utilizing pneumoperitoneum. The posterior peritoneum is dissected, with the liver clearly visible. Anterior dissection is performed close to the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, cystic duct, and common bile duct within the Calot's triangle. The cystic artery is ligated using a ligature, and clips are applied to secure the structures while releasing traction to prevent injury to the common bile duct.

The surgeon applies two clips on the body side and one on the specimen side of the gallbladder. Using a ligature, the gallbladder is carefully removed from the liver bed, noting the thin wall of the gallbladder.
The surgeon demonstrates a bloodless procedure on the gallbladder, with no visible RBCs. The cystic duct is grasped, and its contents, including stones, are suctioned out.
